metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's stated authoritative source from `mcp_server.py` to the actual gate registry (`config.py`'s `_SECTIONS`/`_LEGACY_ENV_ALIASES` or a new dedicated constant), and ensure the CI guard diffs against that location — not mcp_server.py's docstrings/help text.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the REBAR_MCP_* env gates read in mcp_server.py' as the authoritative location for the CI guard's canonical list, but direct file reading shows mcp_server.py only documents these vars in module docstrings and help-text strings. The actual os.environ reads and gate enforcement are distributed across config.py (_LEGACY_ENV_ALIASES + _SECTIONS), _mcp_llm.py, _mcp_reads.py, and _mcp_writes.py. A guard that diffs against mcp_server.py alone would miss gates canonically registered in config.py.
findings.location: Scope section — 'the REBAR_MCP_* env gates read in mcp_server.py / the readonly+allow gates'
findings.suggested_fix: In config.py, define an explicit `MCP_ENV_GATES: frozenset[str] = frozenset({'REBAR_MCP_READONLY', 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM', 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC'})` constant (with the deprecated alias listed separately), and have the CI guard import or grep that constant as its canonical source.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the canonical gate list for the CI guard must span all `_mcp_*.py` modules (not only `mcp_server.py`), or designate a single authoritative location (e.g. a constant in `mcp_server.py`, or `mcp_server.py --help` output) that is machine-readable and kept in sync with the actual gate sites in `_mcp_llm.py`, `_mcp_reads.py`, etc.
findings.finding: The plan states the CI guard will diff against 'the REBAR_MCP_* env gates read in mcp_server.py', but REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM is NOT structurally read or registered in mcp_server.py — it is gated in src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py (call-time os.environ check) and only appears in mcp_server.py's --help string. A guard that parses mcp_server.py as its sole canonical source may miss gates defined in _mcp_llm.py or _mcp_reads.py, producing a fragile drift check.
findings.location: Scope section: 'the canonical gate list in code (the REBAR_MCP_* env gates read in mcp_server.py / the readonly+allow gates)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `REBAR_MCP_GATE_VARS: tuple[str, ...] = ('REBAR_MCP_READONLY', 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM', 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC')` constant in `mcp_server.py` (or a shared location), then have the CI guard compare `server.json`'s `environmentVariables[].name` against that constant — making mcp_server.py the genuine single source of truth rather than a textual --help string.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the CI guard's canonical env-var list is DERIVED by parsing `mcp_server.py` (e.g., grepping for `os.getenv("REBAR_MCP_`)` calls) rather than maintained as a hardcoded set in the script, so the guard self-updates when new gates are added.
findings.finding: The plan says 'Identify the single source of truth for the env gates' but does not commit to HOW the CI guard's canonical list is obtained. If the guard hardcodes the expected env-var set in the script itself (rather than deriving it from mcp_server.py), the guard can itself drift from reality — recreating the exact problem it's meant to prevent.
findings.location: ## What — CI drift-guard; ## Scope — 'Identify the single source of truth for the env gates'
findings.suggested_fix: In the script, dynamically extract REBAR_MCP_* vars from mcp_server.py (e.g., `grep -oP 'REBAR_MCP_[A-Z_]+' mcp_server.py | sort -u`) as the canonical set, then diff against what server.json advertises. Document this derivation approach in a comment in the script.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the CI drift-guard derives the canonical REBAR_MCP_* list programmatically from the config schema's `_SECTIONS['mcp']` keys (via `_canonical_env_name`) and `_LEGACY_ENV_ALIASES` — not from a hardcoded list — so the guard stays self-updating when new MCP gates are added.
findings.finding: The CI drift-guard mechanism is under-specified at the most critical decision point: how it obtains the 'canonical REBAR_MCP_* gate list'. If the guard hardcodes the expected env-var names, it recreates the exact drift problem it is meant to solve — adding a new gate in _config_schema.py's _SECTIONS['mcp'] (e.g. a future REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_X) would not automatically update the guard. The plan says 'Identify the single source of truth' but defers the decision to implementation, leaving open the weaker hardcoded path.
findings.location: Scope section / What section ('Identify the single source of truth for the env gates so the guard has an authoritative list to diff against')
findings.suggested_fix: In the guard script, import and call: `from rebar._config_schema import _SECTIONS; from rebar.config import _LEGACY_ENV_ALIASES, _canonical_env_name` and derive the expected set as `{_canonical_env_name('mcp', k) for k in _SECTIONS['mcp']} | {alias for alias, (sect, _, _) in _LEGACY_ENV_ALIASES.items() if sect == 'mcp'}`. Diff this set against the names in server.json's `environmentVariables` array.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the single source of truth for the canonical `REBAR_MCP_*` gate list (e.g. 'a `GATE_ENV_VARS` constant in `mcp_server.py`' or 'grep/AST-parse all `os.environ.get("REBAR_MCP_*")` calls in `mcp_server.py`') so the CI guard script has an authoritative, non-ambiguous input.
findings.finding: The Scope section says 'Identify the single source of truth for the env gates so the guard has an authoritative list to diff against' — this defers the core design decision (hardcoded list vs. AST-parsed code vs. config constant) to the executor without specifying which approach to take or where the canonical list lives, blocking plan execution.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Identify the single source of truth for the env gates'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to Scope: 'The canonical list is the set of `REBAR_MCP_*` environment variable names read via `os.environ.get` in `mcp_server.py`; the guard script extracts them with a grep/regex and diffs against `server.json`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving step for the failure path of AC3: e.g. 'run the guard script against a deliberately diverged `server.json` fixture (missing `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM`) and assert it exits non-zero' — either as a unit test or an inline CI step.
findings.finding: AC3 ('A CI gate fails when server.json's env-var set diverges') has no described proving command that exercises the failure path — the plan only states the gate passes on the corrected manifest, leaving the failure branch unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'A CI gate fails when server.json's env-var set diverges from the canonical REBAR_MCP_* gate list in code'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the CI gate description: 'Include a negative test: a fixture `server.json` missing one required var is passed to the script and the script must exit 1, confirming the guard catches drift rather than silently passing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence in the 'What' or 'Scope' section clarifying what 'make lint stays green' means in context — e.g., whether the new script must be registered in a Makefile lint target, or whether this is simply asserting no regressions to existing lint rules.
findings.finding: AC4 references 'make lint stays green' as a success criterion, but no section of the plan body describes how the new CI script or server.json change hooks into make lint, what lint checks are involved, or whether lint even covers these files — leaving 'make lint stays green' as an orphan criterion with no described work backing it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4: 'The gate passes on the corrected manifest; make lint stays green.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: 'The new guard script must not introduce lint regressions; if the Makefile has a lint target that runs Python/shell checks, ensure the script is compatible with it (e.g., passes shellcheck or flake8 as appropriate).' This gives the implementer a concrete target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit statement in the plan (Scope or What) confirming whether REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE continues to be honored in server code after this change, and if removal from the manifest is potentially breaking, include a one-line migration note or rollback path for existing users.
findings.finding: The plan removes or deprecates REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE from server.json — a manifest entry that existing users may have configured — without providing an explicit rollback path or expand-contract sequencing if the removal causes user-facing breakage.
findings.location: What section: 'keep REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE only if explicitly labeled a deprecated alias (prefer replacing it with the current name)'; AC2.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE will be removed from server.json; server code currently still reads it (no behavior change), so existing env configs continue to work. If a future PR removes it from server code, that PR must carry its own migration note. Rollback is a one-line revert of server.json.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether `mcp_server.py` still accepts `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` as a runtime alias, and if so, add a comment in server.json (or a migration note in the PR) so existing users know they need not change their env immediately — closing the consumer-retirement gap.
findings.finding: The plan removes (or deprecates) REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE from server.json without enumerating existing consumers of that manifest entry or confirming they require no migration action, leaving the retire path underspecified.
findings.location: What section / AC line 2: 'The deprecated REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE is removed or explicitly marked as a deprecated alias.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan (or PR description), add one sentence: 'The server continues to accept REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE at runtime as an alias; only the manifest hint is being replaced. A follow-up will remove the runtime alias once adoption of the new name is confirmed.' This makes the retire path explicit and safe.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
